title: TypeScript
purpose: Add precise, maintainable static contracts to JavaScript applications and APIs.
outcomes: Explain TypeScript concepts from first principles
Choose when to use and when to avoid important TypeScript features
Implement, test and debug practical TypeScript solutions
Answer conceptual, internal-working and production interview questions

stages.title: Fundamentals
stages.objective: Build an accurate mental model of TypeScript before relying on frameworks or memorized answers.

topics.title: Compiler, inference and annotations
topics.definition: TypeScript adds a structural static type system to JavaScript. It checks contracts during development and erases types when emitting JavaScript.
topics.whyItMatters: It catches incompatible states and preserves relationships across large JavaScript codebases before those errors reach users.
whenToUse: Model API, component and domain contracts
Use unions for a finite set of valid states
Use generics when inputs and outputs share a type relationship
whenNotToUse: Assuming a TypeScript type validates network data at runtime
Using any to silence uncertain data
Encoding business logic in types when runtime code is clearer
internalWorking: The compiler builds types from declarations and inference
Control-flow analysis narrows unions after checks
Structural compatibility compares members rather than type names
Types are erased, so external data still needs runtime validation
tradeoffs: Earlier feedback and safer refactoring versus compiler and modeling cost
Structural typing is flexible but may accept accidentally compatible values
Advanced types can preserve APIs but reduce readability
commonMistakes: Using a type assertion instead of proving a value
Confusing optional with nullable
Forgetting exhaustive handling of a discriminated union
topics.practicalExample: Represent assessment loading as idle | loading | success | error, then switch on status so impossible combinations such as loading with stale error text cannot exist.
topics.diagram:
Compiler, inference and annotations

Requirement and constraints
          |
          v
Validate the input contract
          |
          v
Execute: source is parsed, represented and executed by a compiler, interpreter or runtime
          |
          +--> success -> observable result
          |
          +--> failure -> recover, retry or reject
          |
          v
Measure correctness and cost

interviewQuestions.question: What is Compiler, inference and annotations?
interviewQuestions.answer: TypeScript adds a structural static type system to JavaScript. It checks contracts during development and erases types when emitting JavaScript.
followUps: What changes when the input or traffic for Compiler, inference and annotations grows ten times?

interviewQuestions.question: What problem does Compiler, inference and annotations solve?
interviewQuestions.answer: It catches incompatible states and preserves relationships across large JavaScript codebases before those errors reach users.
followUps: Which failure is most likely in a production TypeScript implementation?

interviewQuestions.question: When should you use Compiler, inference and annotations?
interviewQuestions.answer: Model API, component and domain contracts Use unions for a finite set of valid states Use generics when inputs and outputs share a type relationship
followUps: When would you choose a simpler alternative to Compiler, inference and annotations?

interviewQuestions.question: When should you avoid Compiler, inference and annotations?
interviewQuestions.answer: Assuming a TypeScript type validates network data at runtime Using any to silence uncertain data Encoding business logic in types when runtime code is clearer
followUps: What simpler alternative could replace Compiler, inference and annotations, and what would be lost?

interviewQuestions.question: How does Compiler, inference and annotations work internally?
interviewQuestions.answer: The compiler builds types from declarations and inference Control-flow analysis narrows unions after checks Structural compatibility compares members rather than type names Types are erased, so external data still needs runtime validation
followUps: How would you test and monitor Compiler, inference and annotations?

interviewQuestions.question: What are the main tradeoffs of Compiler, inference and annotations?
interviewQuestions.answer: Earlier feedback and safer refactoring versus compiler and modeling cost Structural typing is flexible but may accept accidentally compatible values Advanced types can preserve APIs but reduce readability
followUps: Which tradeoff matters most under production load?

interviewQuestions.question: Give an interview-ready explanation of Compiler, inference and annotations.
interviewQuestions.answer: TypeScript is a compile-time structural type system for JavaScript. Its types are erased, so it improves local contracts and refactoring but does not validate runtime input. I use unknown at boundaries, runtime schemas for external data and discriminated unions for application states.
followUps: What changes when the input or traffic for Compiler, inference and annotations grows ten times?
Which failure is most likely in a production TypeScript implementation?

topics.whyItMatters: It catches incompatible states and preserves relationships across large JavaScript codebases before those errors reach users.
whenToUse: Model API, component and domain contracts
Use unions for a finite set of valid states
Use generics when inputs and outputs share a type relationship
whenNotToUse: Assuming a TypeScript type validates network data at runtime
Using any to silence uncertain data
Encoding business logic in types when runtime code is clearer
internalWorking: The compiler builds types from declarations and inference
Control-flow analysis narrows unions after checks
Structural compatibility compares members rather than type names
Types are erased, so external data still needs runtime validation
tradeoffs: Earlier feedback and safer refactoring versus compiler and modeling cost
Structural typing is flexible but may accept accidentally compatible values
Advanced types can preserve APIs but reduce readability
commonMistakes: Using a type assertion instead of proving a value
Confusing optional with nullable
Forgetting exhaustive handling of a discriminated union
topics.practicalExample: Represent assessment loading as idle | loading | success | error, then switch on status so impossible combinations such as loading with stale error text cannot exist.

interviewQuestions.question: Give an interview-ready explanation of Use generics to preserve relationships.
interviewQuestions.answer: TypeScript is a compile-time structural type system for JavaScript. Its types are erased, so it improves local contracts and refactoring but does not validate runtime input. I use unknown at boundaries, runtime schemas for external data and discriminated unions for application states.
